sentence: Subjective:

HPI: The patient, a 56-year-old male, presents with a 4-5 week history of difficulty swallowing, describing a sensation of something being stuck in his throat, which is more pronounced with solids than liquids.
He reports a little pain and has considered choking, but has not fully choked.
He has not noticed any blood, coughing, weight loss, or chest pain.
The patient has a past medical history of diabetes and high blood pressure.
Past medical history: The patient has diabetes and high blood pressure, for which he is taking metformin 500mg twice a day and norvasc, respectively.
Review of systems: The patient reports no nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, or other side effects from his medications.
Current medications:
Metformin 500mg twice a day for diabetes
Norvasc (dose not specified) for high blood pressure
Objective:
Vital signs: The patient's blood pressure was 120/50.
Physical exam: The patient's neck was fine, with no swelling or masses.
The thyroid was normal, with no masses or lymph nodes.
The throat examination was normal, with no masses, swelling, or redness.
The lung and heart exams were clear, with no murmurs.
The belly exam revealed epigastric tenderness on the right side, but no masses or significant swelling.
The back and extremity exams were normal.
The neurological exam was fine.
Test Results: [NOT MENTIONED]
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient is experiencing difficulty swallowing, which may be due to a narrowing of the esophagus, potentially a stricture.
Problem list: Difficulty swallowing (active); Diabetes (active); Hypertension (active); Reflux (suspected)
Plan:
I prescribed Prilosec 20mg once a day for reflux.
I recommended that the patient chew his foods frequently and thoroughly, especially with hard-to-swallow foods.
I referred the patient to gastroenterology for a scope to investigate the cause of the difficulty swallowing.
I also ordered a repeat hemoglobin A1c and additional blood work to check kidney function.
I referred the patient to ophthalmology for an eye examination to check for diabetic retinopathy.
I continued the patient's current medications, including metformin and norvasc.
Follow-up: The patient will return in about a month for a follow-up appointment to discuss the results of the blood tests and any necessary adjustments to his treatment plan.
